expectations.text: A0: Agent produced both output files with real content
expectations.evidence: analysis.md (98 lines) and architecture.md (57 lines) both contain substantive content

expectations.text: A1: Correct architecture recommendation (Service-Based with evolution)
expectations.evidence: Selected: Service-Based Architecture with evolution path toward selective Microservices. Justified by 4-dev team, moderate budget, and need to start simple.

expectations.text: A2: Ranked comparison of ALL 8 styles
expectations.evidence: Table in Step 2 ranks all 8: Microservices, Service-Based, Event-Driven, Space-Based, Microkernel, Service-Oriented, Layered, Modular Monolith

expectations.text: A3: Fitness function table with style-specific structural FFs (not just characteristic FFs)
expectations.evidence: 4 structural FFs for Phase 1: Service boundary alignment (services align with domain boundaries), Limited service count (4-12 range), No service-to-service chatter (call limits per request), Database sharing discipline (each service accesses only its own tables/views). All are structural invariants of service-based architecture.

expectations.text: A4: Style fitness functions are specific to the selected style (service-based)
expectations.evidence: FFs enforce service-based-specific invariants: service count within 4-12 range (coarser than microservices), database sharing discipline (shared DB with scoped access — different from microservices' 'no shared DB'), service-to-service chatter limits. These differ from both microservices FFs and modular monolith FFs.

expectations.text: A5: Fitness functions include Tool/Approach column
expectations.evidence: Tool/Approach column present: Directory/package structure check, Service registry/count check, Runtime tracing or static call graph, SQL analysis/ORM scope check

expectations.text: A6: Fitness functions scoped to current phase, future FFs mentioned but not mandatory
expectations.evidence: Phase 1 FFs clearly labeled as 'Current Phase: Phase 1 — Service-Based'. Note explicitly states: 'Only Phase 1 (Service-Based) fitness functions are active. When the team transitions to Phase 2, Microservices fitness functions will be added.' Phase 2/3 transitions have measurable triggers.
